articles.headline: Senate Committee Moves to Restrict EV Tax Credits for Foreign-Assembled Vehicles
articles.body: The Senate Finance Committee approved a measure that would restrict electric vehicle tax credits to cars assembled in the United States, a move that could eliminate incentives for roughly half of EVs currently on the market. The proposal targets vehicles assembled in Europe and Asia, citing concerns about supply chain dependency on foreign lithium processing. Automakers including Ford and GM expressed cautious support while foreign brands warned of retaliatory trade measures.

articles.headline: EU Unveils Mandatory Carbon Reporting Standards for Automakers Starting 2026
articles.body: The European Commission formally adopted mandatory lifecycle carbon reporting requirements for all passenger vehicles sold in the EU, effective January 2026. The regulation requires manufacturers to disclose full supply chain emissions including battery production and raw material extraction. EV makers initially praised the move but flagged concerns about the compressed timeline for compliance infrastructure.

articles.headline: Lithium Triangle Nations Form Export Coordination Body Amid Western Pressure
articles.body: Argentina, Bolivia, and Chile announced the formation of a joint lithium export coordination body, drawing comparisons to OPEC. The bloc controls roughly 60 percent of global lithium reserves and cited the need to secure fair pricing as Western demand for EV batteries accelerates. Markets reacted cautiously with lithium carbonate futures rising 4 percent on the news. Analysts warned the move could accelerate US and EU investment in domestic lithium extraction.

articles.headline: Toyota and Panasonic Announce Solid-State Battery Joint Venture for 2027 Production
articles.body: Toyota Motor Corporation and Panasonic Holdings confirmed a joint venture targeting commercial solid-state battery production by 2027. The new cells promise energy density improvements of 40 percent over current lithium-ion technology and significantly faster charging times. The announcement pushed Toyota shares up 3.2 percent in Tokyo trading. Industry analysts noted the timeline is aggressive but credible given both companies combined IP in battery chemistry.

articles.headline: TSMC Arizona Fab Faces 18-Month Delay as Equipment Suppliers Cite Export License Backlog
articles.body: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company's Arizona fabrication facility faces an 18-month delay in reaching full production capacity, sources familiar with the project confirmed. The bottleneck centers on export license approvals for advanced lithography equipment from ASML, which requires US Commerce Department sign-off under expanded chip export controls. The delay threatens to push full production past the 2026 target that anchored federal CHIPS Act subsidies.

articles.headline: South Korea Warns US Chip Restrictions Risk Fragmenting Global Semiconductor Ecosystem
articles.body: South Korea's trade ministry issued a formal diplomatic note warning that escalating US semiconductor export controls risk fragmenting the global chip supply chain in ways that would harm allied nations as much as adversaries. The note cited downstream impacts on Samsung and SK Hynix memory production, which relies on US-origin equipment and software. Washington responded that targeted controls are necessary to prevent military applications of advanced chips.

articles.headline: ASML Reports Record Backlog as AI Chip Demand Overwhelms EUV Production Capacity
articles.body: ASML Holding reported a record order backlog of 39 billion euros as demand for extreme ultraviolet lithography machines continues to exceed manufacturing capacity. The Dutch equipment maker cited AI infrastructure buildout as the primary demand driver, with hyperscalers accounting for a growing share of orders. ASML warned that delivery times for new EUV systems now extend to 24 months, creating a structural bottleneck for global semiconductor capacity expansion.

articles.headline: China Restricts Gallium and Germanium Exports in Escalating Tech Trade Dispute
articles.body: China's Ministry of Commerce announced new export licensing requirements for gallium and germanium, critical materials used in semiconductor manufacturing and fiber optic production. The move follows US restrictions on advanced chip exports to Chinese entities and is widely interpreted as a countermeasure. European chipmakers warned the restrictions could force production halts within months if alternative supply sources cannot be qualified.

articles.headline: EU AI Act Enforcement Agency Names First Director, Begins Rulemaking for High-Risk Systems
articles.body: The European AI Office formally named its first executive director and announced the start of a 90-day public consultation on technical standards for high-risk AI systems under the EU AI Act. The Act classifies AI applications in critical infrastructure, hiring, credit scoring, and law enforcement as high-risk, requiring mandatory conformity assessments before deployment. Industry groups praised the consultation process but warned the compliance timeline remains unclear.

articles.headline: OpenAI Faces FTC Investigation Over Deceptive AI Output Claims in Marketing Materials
articles.body: The Federal Trade Commission opened a formal investigation into OpenAI's marketing claims about the accuracy and reliability of its AI systems, sources familiar with the matter confirmed. The probe centers on whether consumer-facing claims about ChatGPT's capabilities constitute deceptive trade practices under FTC Act Section 5. OpenAI issued a statement welcoming regulatory engagement and said it would cooperate fully with the investigation.

articles.headline: Anthropic Publishes Model Specification Document Detailing Constitutional AI Constraints
articles.body: Anthropic released a comprehensive model specification document describing the behavioral constraints and value alignment approach embedded in its Claude AI systems. The document outlines a hierarchy of priorities including safety, ethical behavior, and helpfulness, and describes how conflicts between these priorities are resolved. The publication was praised by AI safety researchers as a rare example of meaningful transparency from a frontier AI lab.

articles.headline: G7 Nations Agree Framework for Cross-Border AI Data Governance
articles.body: G7 leaders endorsed a non-binding framework for cross-border AI data governance at their summit in Hiroshima, establishing principles for data sharing between allied nations' AI research programs. The framework includes provisions for mutual recognition of safety evaluations and a joint early-warning system for emerging AI risks. Critics noted the voluntary nature of the commitments limits their practical effect.

articles.headline: Nvidia Blackwell GPU Shortages Force Cloud Providers to Delay AI Infrastructure Rollouts
articles.body: Major cloud service providers including Amazon Web Services, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud are experiencing significant delays in AI infrastructure expansion due to constrained supply of Nvidia's Blackwell architecture GPUs. Sources at two of the three providers indicated delivery timelines have slipped by six to nine months. Nvidia attributed the shortage to unprecedented demand rather than manufacturing constraints and guided for supply improvement by mid-2026.

articles.headline: IEA Report: Global EV Sales to Reach 20 Million Units in 2025, Outpacing Grid Infrastructure
articles.body: The International Energy Agency projected global electric vehicle sales will reach 20 million units in 2025, representing 25 percent of all new car sales. The agency warned that grid infrastructure investment is not keeping pace with adoption, particularly in developing markets where charging networks remain sparse. The report called for coordinated public investment in grid modernization alongside continued EV incentive programs.

articles.headline: BYD Surpasses Tesla in Global EV Sales for Third Consecutive Quarter
articles.body: BYD Co. surpassed Tesla for the third consecutive quarter in global electric vehicle unit sales, selling 1.1 million vehicles against Tesla's 890,000 in the third quarter. BYD's dominance was concentrated in China and Southeast Asian markets where its lower price points and extensive dealer networks provide structural advantages. Tesla maintained its lead in the North American premium segment and cited strong order growth for its Cybertruck.

articles.headline: California Accelerates Zero-Emission Vehicle Mandate After Federal Rollback Attempt Fails
articles.body: California's Air Resources Board finalized its Advanced Clean Cars II regulation requiring 100 percent zero-emission new vehicle sales by 2035, after a federal attempt to preempt state authority under the Clean Air Act was rejected by the Ninth Circuit. Fifteen states that follow California's emission standards will automatically adopt the rule, representing roughly 40 percent of the US auto market. Automakers said the ruling provides long-awaited regulatory certainty.

articles.headline: Intel Loses TSMC Foundry Contract for Next-Generation Server Chips to Samsung
articles.body: Intel Corporation lost a foundry contract for its next-generation Xeon server processor line to Samsung Foundry, industry sources confirmed. The decision reflects continued yield concerns at Intel's 18A process node, which has faced persistent defect rate issues. The loss is a significant blow to Intel's IDM 2.0 strategy, which positioned external foundry customers as a key revenue driver for its manufacturing division.

articles.headline: EU Parliament Pushes for Stricter Liability Rules in AI Act Delegated Acts
articles.body: Members of the European Parliament submitted formal amendments calling for strict liability provisions in the delegated acts implementing the EU AI Act, which would hold AI developers financially responsible for harms caused by high-risk systems without requiring proof of negligence. The amendments go beyond the Commission's preferred fault-based liability approach and face significant opposition from US and European tech industry lobbying groups.

articles.headline: Rare Earth Processing Bottleneck Emerges as Critical Constraint for Semiconductor and EV Supply Chains
articles.body: A rare earth element processing bottleneck is emerging as a critical shared constraint for both semiconductor and electric vehicle supply chains, according to a new analysis from the Rocky Mountain Institute. China currently controls over 85 percent of global rare earth processing capacity, creating a single point of failure across multiple strategic industries. The analysis called for urgent investment in Western processing infrastructure, estimating a minimum eight-year runway to meaningful diversification.

articles.headline: DeepMind AlphaFold 3 Enables Breakthrough in Solid-State Electrolyte Discovery
articles.body: Researchers at Stanford University and Argonne National Laboratory used DeepMind's AlphaFold 3 protein structure prediction system to identify three novel solid-state electrolyte candidates with ionic conductivity exceeding current best-in-class materials. The discovery, published in Nature, was hailed as a demonstration of AI's potential to compress materials science research timelines from decades to years. The electrolytes are potentially applicable to both EV batteries and grid-scale energy storage.

articles.headline: Japan's METI Launches 10 Billion Yen Fund for Domestic Semiconductor Materials R&D
articles.body: Japan's Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry announced a 10 billion yen research fund targeting domestic development of advanced semiconductor materials including photoresists, high-k dielectrics, and chemical mechanical planarization slurries. The fund reflects Japan's strategic positioning as a materials and equipment supplier to global chipmakers, and comes amid growing concern about supply chain dependencies exposed by the pandemic.

articles.headline: US FTC Finalizes Rule Banning Non-Compete Agreements, Signals Scrutiny of AI Talent Lock-In
articles.body: The Federal Trade Commission finalized a rule banning most non-compete agreements across US industries, and in its accompanying commentary specifically flagged AI talent mobility as an area of ongoing concern. The FTC indicated it would scrutinize IP assignment clauses and garden leave provisions used by AI companies to effectively restrict researchers from joining competitors. The rule faces multiple legal challenges from business groups.

articles.headline: Volkswagen Announces European Battery Gigafactory Delays Amid Demand Slowdown
articles.body: Volkswagen Group confirmed delays to two of its planned European battery gigafactories, citing slower-than-expected EV demand growth and financing pressures. The Salzgitter facility, originally slated for 2025 production, has been pushed to 2027. The company maintained its long-term EV transition strategy but acknowledged the pace of consumer adoption has trailed internal projections in Germany and France.

articles.headline: Baidu Launches Ernie 4.0 with Claims of GPT-4 Parity on Chinese Language Benchmarks
articles.body: Baidu Inc. launched Ernie 4.0, its latest large language model, claiming performance parity with GPT-4 on Chinese-language reasoning and comprehension benchmarks. Independent researchers noted the comparison methodology favored tasks where Chinese-language training data density provides a structural advantage. The launch reflects intensifying competition in the Chinese AI market, where Alibaba, ByteDance, and Tencent are also fielding frontier models.

articles.headline: IEA Warns Global Critical Mineral Investment Falling Short of Energy Transition Needs
articles.body: The International Energy Agency published a special report warning that global investment in critical mineral mining and processing is running at roughly 60 percent of the level needed to support the energy transition under the IEA's Net Zero pathway. Lithium, cobalt, nickel, and rare earths all face projected supply deficits by 2030 under current investment trajectories. The agency called for coordinated public-private financing mechanisms and faster permitting reform in Western mining jurisdictions.
